coredata.dc:description: Mild steel materials are extensively used in various industrial operations in acidic conditions, but it susceptible to corrosion and lose its service life. Decalepis hamiltonii root extract (DHRE) containing several phytochemicals are readily interact on mild steel surface and prevent it from corrosion. The weight loss method, potentiodynamic polarization and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy revealed that, DHRE inhibited maximum 97.19%, 97.35%, and 96.73%, respectively for corrosion of MS in 1 M HCl. Activation energy in the absence of the inhibitor was found to 80.89 kJ/mol, which increased to 140.55 kJ/mol with 200 ppm of DHRE concentration, indicating a higher energy requirement to undergo corrosion Scanning electron microscopy images of MS surface in presence of DHRE constituents, serving as a safeguard for MS corrosion. Atomic force microscopic images shows the corroded MS surface roughness in presence of DHRE reduced from 1048 nm to 682 nm. The variation of MS surface hydrophilic character was evaluated by contact angle measurements.

head.ce:title: “Evaluation of surface interaction of plant root extract components on mild steel surface in HCl medium: Electrochemical, and surface characterization approaches”

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Mild steel materials are extensively used in various industrial operations in acidic conditions, but it susceptible to corrosion and lose its service life. Decalepis hamiltonii root extract (DHRE) containing several phytochemicals are readily interact on mild steel surface and prevent it from corrosion. The weight loss method, potentiodynamic polarization and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy revealed that, DHRE inhibited maximum 97.19%, 97.35%, and 96.73%, respectively for corrosion of MS in 1 M HCl. Activation energy in the absence of the inhibitor was found to 80.89 kJ/mol, which increased to 140.55 kJ/mol with 200 ppm of DHRE concentration, indicating a higher energy requirement to undergo corrosion Scanning electron microscopy images of MS surface in presence of DHRE constituents, serving as a safeguard for MS corrosion. Atomic force microscopic images shows the corroded MS surface roughness in presence of DHRE reduced from 1048 nm to 682 nm. The variation of MS surface hydrophilic character was evaluated by contact angle measurements.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Mild steel has been universally employed in numerous manufacturing factories since long ago, which including shipbuilding, civil engineering, automobiles, and home appliances (Behzadnasab et al., 2011). Aqueous acids are frequently utilized in industrial operations for cleaning, pickling, and descaling, particularly in acidification procedures and oilfields (Tan et al., 2018, 2019). These acidic solutions severely corrode metal surfaces, resulting in significant financial losses for numerous sectors. Consequently, it is critical to stop and combat rusting, and it appears, in our opinion, that changing the solution's condition could protect the material from corrosion. Because of their affordability, effectiveness, and ease of use, inhibitors are frequently employed (Hegazy et al., 2012; Lagrenee et al., 2002). Inhibitors are regularly utilized to stop the aggressive assault on metallic items and to stop metal disintegration and acid consumption. In the industrial setting, inhibitors are chemical substances that are employed in tiny amounts to slow down the rate at which metals and alloys corrode when exposed to harsh environments, and so reduce economic losses from metallic corrosion by delaying the corrosion process and limiting its rate. The way inhibitors work is heavily dependent on how they are formulated and how wisely they are used in certain situations. Researchers are looking at eco-friendly corrosion inhibitors since some unconventional materials can be utilized instead of synthesised, dangerous corrosion inhibitors. Due to the growing demand for green chemistry, the hunt for an effective inhibitor for metal corrosion in various corrosive conditions has recently gained increased importance. Furthermore, the material to be employed as a metal corrosion inhibitor needs to be affordable, easily accessible, and ecologically friendly because the entire concept of metal protection is based on financial benefit and ecological durability. Plants are a good source of inexpensive, easily accessible, and safe green inhibitors. Plant components have an organic composition and include specific phytoconstituents. Numerous reports have indicated that plant extracts from diverse sections are good and efficient metal corrosion inhibitors in a variety of corrosive conditions (Owate et al., 2014; Ji et al., 2011; Krishnegowda et al., 2013). The current study details how mild steel is inhibited by root extracts of Decalepis hamiltonii in HCl. The common name of this plant is “swallow root”. Decalepis hamiltonii is a plant whose root is used to make pickles and syrup. Many illnesses, including those of the digestive, pulmonary, and circulatory systems, particularly these root tubers, are employed in Ayurvedic, Siddha, and traditional medical treatments as a common vitalizer, blood purifier, wound healer, and treatment for respiratory disorders, high temperature, internal drain, ignis sacer (erysipelas), poisoning, and pediatric rejuvenation (Ragini et al., 2010). Numerous publications exist concerning the application of root extracts in MS corrosion investigations conducted in an acidic medium, such as Valeriana willichi roots (Haldhar et al., 2018), Olive roots (Bouknana et al., 2015), waste feverfew root (Zhou et al., 2023), Rheum ribes root extract (Kaya et al., 2023a), Chlorophytum borivilianum Root Extract (Ji et al., 2013), Phyllanthus mallerianus (Nsude and Orie, 2022), etc. Thus, plant root extracts have been extensively studied as corrosion inhibitors. In addition to the roots, other parts of the plant, such as flowers, bark, leaves, and stems, also act as effective corrosion inhibitors. The study of plant extracts as corrosion inhibitors for mild steel has shown promising results across various experimental conditions. For instance, Bougainvillea glabra extract demonstrated a significant inhibition rate of 93.13 ± 0.02% at a concentration of 250 ppm in a 1.0 M H2SO4 solution (Kumar et al., 2023). Similarly, research on Randia monantha benth extract revealed an inhibition efficiency of 91% at a concentration of 1000 ppm in a 1 M HCl solution (Carmona-Hernandez et al., 2023). Furthermore, Althaea officinalis extract was identified as a sustainable and green inhibitor, achieving a protection efficiency of 92.82% for carbon steel in a 0.5 M HCl solution at 800 ppm (Iranpour et al., 2023).

Morinda citrifolia extract exhibited an inhibition efficiency of 85.10% at a concentration of 300 mg/L, using the potentiodynamic polarization (PDP) technique (Royani et al., 2024a). Additionally, Dillenia suffruticosa leaves extract showed an inhibition efficiency of 81.4% in a 1 M HCl solution at 1000 ppm (Mamudu et al., 2023). In subsequent studies, Origanum compactum extract achieved a 90% inhibition efficiency in a 1 M HCl solution at a concentration of 400 mg/L at 303K (Berrissoul et al., 2022). Similarly, Vitex agnus-castus leaves extract demonstrated an inhibition efficiency of 81.05% in a 1 M HCl solution at 1 g/L at 298K (Hamdouch et al., 2023). Wood hibiscus leaf extract achieved a notable inhibition efficiency of 93% in a 0.5 M H2SO4 solution at a concentration of 160 mg/L at 298K (Xu et al., 2023). These findings collectively underscore the potential of various plant extracts as effective, environmentally friendly corrosion inhibitors for mild steel in acidic environments. The high inhibition efficiencies observed in these studies highlight the potential of using natural extracts for sustainable corrosion protection in industrial applications. This work therefore presents the formation of biologically inspired inhibitors in an effort to promote the usage of feasible corrosion inhibitors and boost the commercial viability of these products. Since the roots of the Decalepis hamiltonii plant is an indigenous species, the inhibitor that is produced is both ecologically beneficial and biodegradable. In order to extract DHRE using the least quantity of solvent feasible, the Soxhlet extraction method was employed. Taking several electro-active components from the root of Decalepis hamiltonii and applying them as a possible corrosion inhibitor for MS in 1 M HCl is the primary goal of the current study (Rathod et al., 2022).

To determine the primary categories of active chemical compounds found in the Decalepis hamiltonii root tuber extract, a phytochemical screening was conducted. Simple qualitative techniques were used to test for the existence of saponins, alkaloids, tannins, steroids, terpenoids, phenolic compounds, flavonoids, glycosides, and phenolic compounds (Boominathan et al., 2018).

Decalepis hamiltonii roots were collected from Khanapur in the Belagavi District of India. Fig. 1 a shows a root-bearing Decalepis hamiltonii plant, while Fig. 1b shows powdered and dried root material. After being washed, the plant roots were shade-dried for 25 days and pulverized. In a Soxhlet extractor, 400 mL of methanol was used to extract 30g of powdered material over the course of 5 h. Plant extracts were preserved in desiccators after their solvents were evaporated. DHRE was used to prepare the inhibitor solution.

The samples are subjected to a corrosive solution consisting of a 1.0 M HCl solution. An inhibitor solution was formed by diluting a different range of DHRE solutions in 1 M HCl. Reagents of AR grade and double-distilled water were utilized to create corrosive liquid samples.

The MS plate was cut into dimensions of 5 cm × 1 cm × 0.2 cm for the weight loss technique and 1 cm2 for polarization studies. The chemical composition of the MS was as follows (in percentage): Mn 0.27, Si 0.07, S 0.03, P 0.06, Ni 0.02, Cu 0.02, C 0.10, with the remainder being Fe. The samples were polished using silicon carbide papers ranging from 180 to 2000 grit, then degreased with acetone, rinsed with distilled water, and dried with a clean cloth. The initial weight of the samples was measured using an analytical balance.

GC Clarus 500 PerkinElmer system was employed to assess the ethanol extract of Decalepis hemiltoni via gas chromatography coupled with mass spectrometry (GC-MS). The system utilized a capillary column (30.0 × 0.25 μmID × 0.25 μmID) composed of an Elite-5MS (5% diphenyl and 95% dimethyl polysiloxane) in conjunction with a GC-MS. GC-MS detection was conducted using an electron ionization system with a 70-eV ionization energy. Throughout this investigation, a Turbo-Mass Gold-Perkin-Elmer mass detector and Turbo-mass version 5.2 software were employed for the analysis of mass spectra and chromatograms.

The prevention of corrosion was investigated using the weight loss technique. The MS coupons were suspended in a beaker containing 100 mL of aqueous HCl, both with and without varying doses of the DHRE inhibitor. After the test, coupons were washed, dried, and weighed. They were then stored in a desiccator until they were again weighed. The mean results of weight loss were also documented in the same way.

To evaluate electrochemical impedance spectroscopy and polarization, the CHIE-1660 electrochemical workstation software was used at ambient temperature. Polarization was determined using a configuration of three electrode cells. The mild steel surface of the cell acts as a working electrode for polarization measurements. Platinum and silver-silver chloride electrodes are used as the appropriate reference and counter electrodes. A polished working electrode with a 1 cm2 exposed surface area was utilized for the electrochemical experiment (the remaining area was covered with epoxy). After 35 min of allowing the working electrode to be in the test solution, the stability of the OCP was confirmed. The 200-mV potential range and 1 mV s−1 scan rate were used to create the OCP polarization curves. Nyquist plots were obtained over a frequency range of 100 kHz to 0.1 Hz to analyze the electrochemical behavior of the system, with amplitude of 5 mV s−1. Z-simp 3.21 uses Nyquist impedance values to fit the applicable circuit (Minagalavar et al., 2023).

Using conventional equations, the weight loss technique was utilized to detect the activation parameter and adsorption nature for MS corrosion at temperatures ranging from 300 ± 1 to 340±1K, both the existence and nonexistence of DHRE components.

With a wavelength of 4000 to 400 cm−1 for DHRE, a Nicolet 5700 FT-IR spectrometer was utilized. FT-IR spectra were acquired in order to recognize the functional groups and how these groups correlated with one another on the MS barrier at the time of acid corrosion.

The methods of SEM, AFM, and WCA were utilized to examine the surface morphologies of mild steel. (SEM-Model-JEOL make, JSM-IT 500LA; AFM-NanosurfR EasyScan 2; WCA-Model-DMs-401 Famas software make: Kyowa Interface Science Co. Ltd., Japan). To obtain pictures of the functioning MS electrode surface, the specimen was immersed in the test solution for 5 h at ambient temperature, both with and without DHRE.
A phytochemical analysis of the DHRE revealed the presence of alkaloids, tannins, steroids, terpenoids, phenolic compounds, flavonoids, glycosides, and saponins, as detailed in Table 1 .

Gas chromatography mass spectrometry is an analytical approach employed to identify the diverse biological components present in crude plant extracts. These components encompass amino acids, alcoholic compounds, elongated hydrocarbons, ester-based compounds, alkanes, volatile substances, and similar constituents. The information acquired from GC-MS analysis, including duration of retention, peak region, and chemical formula, was utilized to confirm the presence of phytochemicals in the plant extract. Fig. 2 a and b depict the major and highly active constituents identified in the DHRE through the GC-MS test method, and Fig. 2 c represents the chromatogram of the DHRE obtained from the GC-MS approach. Table 2 provides a full list of the ten main active components, which includes information such as retention time, molecular weight, empirical formula, and concentration (%). Forty-two phytoconstituents were identified in the DHRE plant through GC-MS analysis. The principal substances identified and responsible for inhibition are d-glycero-d-tallo-heptose, 1-(7-Hydroxy-1,6,6-trimethyl-10-oxatricyclo [5.2.1.0 (2,4)] dec-9-yl) ethenone, 9-Octadecenal etc.

The acid dissolution behavior of MS in 1M HCl was examined using mass reduction and electrochemical techniques, both in the presence and absence of DHRE. The MS dissolve rate was found to be quite high when 1M HCl was used alone; however, the dissolution rate was significantly decreased when an inhibitor was added (Ji et al., 2012).

The dissolution rate and % inhibition performance of MS in an aqueous acid at 300 ± 1 K with various DHRE doses and different time hours are shown in Table 3 . Corrosion will occur more quickly in the absence of an inhibitor due to the plenty of H+ ions available in the acid medium, which speeds up the reduction process. MS corrosion is reduced by DHRE in comparison to acidic media, and a raise in inhibitor concentration improves inhibitory efficacy. At an optimal DHRE dose of 200 ppm for a 1.0 M HCl solution, the highest efficiency of 97.23% was observed. 200 ppm was determined to be the ideal inhibitor dosage because no discernible increase in efficacy was seen when the inhibitor concentration was raised. If the concentration of the inhibitor is higher than 200 ppm, the inhibitor-metal connections may become weaker, and H2O or Cl− ions may replace the inhibitor, which will reduce the inhibitor's effectiveness. In an acidic solution, the liberation of hydrogen also continues to be dominant. Therefore, mass accumulation is not occurring throughout the superficial layer of MS. In an acidic solution, the DHRE constituents undergo protonation, which results in a decrease in the reduction reaction. When DHRE components are added, they attach to the mild steel's surface and form a thin coating that keeps the metal surface isolated from acidic environments and prevents corrosion. For consistency, each experiment was replicated thrice. The mean and standard deviation were calculated. Eqs. (1)–(3) are formulas to evaluate inhibition efficiency (%IE), corrosion rate, and surface coverage.

W1 and W2 reflect the weight reduction of MS in the existence and nonexistence of DHRE inhibitors, respectively.

The changing protection ability of the corrosion inhibitor over time is a critical aspect of the study. Table 3 illustrates this phenomenon in detail, showing how the inhibition efficiency evolves with prolonged exposure. Initially, the inhibition efficiency is high due to the rapid adsorption of DHRE molecules on the MS surface, forming a protective film. However, over time, there is a gradual decrease in inhibition efficiency. The decline in protection efficiency can be attributed to several factors. As time progresses, the concentration of active DHRE molecules in the solution may reduce due to continuous adsorption and possible degradation, leading to a thinner protective layer. Additionally, the formation of corrosion products on the SCS surface can impede further adsorption of inhibitor molecules, thus reducing the effectiveness of the protective film. Variations in temperature and pH over time can also affect the stability and adsorption efficiency of the inhibitor molecules.

The electrochemical potential state of a material with regard to its electrolytes over time is revealed by Open Circuit Potential (OCP) measurements. The OCP is defined as the free potential that exists between reference and working electrodes when no current flows through the circuit. OCP measurements were used to record the electrochemical behavior, including impedance and polarization measurements, of MS in 1 M HCl solution for both untreated and treated samples. The OCP-time curves for MS in 1 M HCl solution containing different concentrations of DHRE are shown in Fig. 3 . As illustrated in Fig. 2, the initial open circuit potential of the SCS electrode without the inhibitor was −0.495 V and progressively shifted to more positive values −0.47 V over time. The presence of DHRE shifts the potential value into the positive range compared to the untreated sample. The OCP values for treated and untreated samples vary slightly over time. After a certain period, the potential-time curves for both treated and untreated MS specimens tend to form a straight line, indicating a steady-state potential (Dehghani et al., 2019).

The polarization curves of MS in a 1.0 M HCl solution are displayed in Fig. 4 both in the absence and with various inhibitor concentrations. Eq. (4) is utilized to calculate the inhibition efficiency for each inhibitor concentration.

Here, i 0
corr and i corr symbolize corrosion current densities without and with various DHRE doses. Table 4 lists the electrochemical characteristics, including cathodic (bc) and anodic (ba) Tafel plots, inhibition efficiency (%η
w ), corrosion potential (Ecorr), and corrosion current density (icorr). Each experiment was conducted three times for each test group under identical conditions. The consistency of the results was confirmed by the standard deviation of the electrochemical polarization measurements. Lower corrosion current densities are achieved by raising inhibitor concentrations. Fig. 4 and Table 4 clearly demonstrate that the presence of DHRE in the acidic medium progressively reduces both cathodic and anodic current densities. This effect becomes increasingly pronounced with higher concentrations of DHRE (Solmaz et al., 2023). This behavior demonstrates the inhibitor's ability to prevent MS corrosion in acidic medium and by chemically adsorbing its molecules on both anodic and cathodic sites, which facilitates that it functions through a mixed mode of inhibition. Table 4 shows the relationship between inhibitor concentration and the decrease in corrosion current density (Salcı et al., 2022). Fig. 4 illustrates that the presence of an inhibitor causes a significant shift in both cathodic and anodic polarization curves toward lower current densities. This shift is more pronounced in the cathodic Tafel curves due to the inhibitor reducing hydrogen evolution, and in the anodic Tafel curves due to decreased metal dissolution (Oulabbas et al., 2022; Sedik et al., 2020). These findings indicate effective corrosion mitigation. From Table 4 it is evident that the presence of the inhibitor significantly reduces the anodic Tafel slopes (from 137 mV/dec to 76 mV/dec), indicating that the inhibitor affects the anodic reaction mechanism. This suggests that the inhibitor not only blocks the superficial reaction sites but also alters the anodic reaction mechanism. The reduction in the corrosion rate for mild steel when an inhibitor is added to the corrosive medium supports this conclusion. The data in Table 4 elucidates that as the inhibitor concentration increases, the corrosion rate of the metal decreases in 1M HCl at a temperature of 300 ± 1 K. These results prompt the inference that DHRE holds promise as a potent inhibitor (Hosseini and Azimi, 2009). The examined inhibitors appear to behave as mixed-type agents, according to the overall data. It has been noted that when inhibitor concentrations rise, inhibition efficiency also increases. Maximum inhibitory efficiency is achieved at a concentration of 200 ppm, with a maximum value of 97.35%.

An essential non-destructive technique for evaluating the protective layer that develops on metallic surfaces and providing an estimate of the mechanism causing corrosion are done by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy measurement (El Hattak et al., 2021). EIS studies enable a quantitative comprehension of the kinetics of the electrode process and the surface characteristics at the metal/solution contact. EIS provides a quick and simple method for monitoring the track of corrosion processes, with evaluation carried out close to the corrosion potential to ensure accurate results. The fact that EIS operates at lower applied voltage levels makes non-destructive tests possible. Nyquist plots of the EIS results for mild steel subjected to 1 M HCl solution at 300 ± 1 K are shown in Fig. 5 a., both with and without varying concentrations of DHRE (Mobin and Aslam, 2017; Garai et al., 2012; Bahrami et al., 2010). According to the Nyquist plots a simple Randles electrical equivalent circuit diagram was proposed on Fig. 5b and the data obtained by fitting the experimental results to these circuit diagrams are given on Table 5 . Where, CPE is constant phase element, n is the phase shift, Rs is solution resistance, Rp is polarization resistance which contains charge transfer resistance (Rct), double layer capacitance (Cdl), chi-squared value and observed % inhibition efficiency (%ηw). By the following relationship, the % inhibition efficiency (%ηw) was evaluated (Tian et al., 2018).

The Nyquist plots depicted in Fig. 5a strongly indicate the significant influence of DHRE on the corrosion rate. This influence is distinguished by analysing the diameters of the semicircles and comparing those observed in the presence of the DHRE with those in the uninhibited solution. Notably, as the DHRE concentration increases to 200 ppm, a maximum of 97.09 % η
w was attained. The experiment was conducted three times for each test group under identical conditions, and the standard deviation of electrochemical impedance findings confirmed consistent results. Inhibition performance (%η
w ) was computed and determined based on electrochemical impedance studies. Furthermore, the Nyquist plots exhibit irregular, single-depressed semicircles or instances of frequency dispersion. This phenomenon, commonly encountered in solid electrodes, results from surface irregularities and roughness on the electrode's surface. A comprehensive analysis of the electrochemical impedance variables, deduced through fitting experimental outcomes to the applied electrochemical circuit model (Fig. 5b.). DHRE controls corrosion through adsorption at the metal/solution interface, which is validated by the fact that C
dl readings reduce as DHRE concentration increases. This behavior is attributed to a rise in the electrical double layer's thickness. The rise in R
p readings with DHRE concentration implies a rise in charge transfer resistance. The diameter of the depressed semi-circle is related to R
p
, which increases as the concentration increases up to 200 ppm (Kaya et al., 2023b). The values of polarization resistance R
p are represented as semicircles centered on the real part of the Nyquist plot. It was observed that the polarization resistance increased significantly after the addition of various amounts of DHRE. The fact that the capacitive loop width widened as DHRE concentration increased offers more support to this concept. Polarization resistance (R
p
) is equal to the sum of the charge transfer resistance (R
ct
). As the R
p value increases, the Cdl value decreases with increasing inhibitor concentration, indicating the adsorption of active molecules on the surface (Pai et al., 2024).

However, the value of Cdl decreases with increasing inhibitor concentration. This phenomenon can be explained by the Helmholtz model, which suggests that the double-layer capacitance is inversely proportional to surface changes (

where εo is the permittivity of space, ε is the local dielectric constant, d is the film thickness, and S is the surface area. According to Eq. (6), the decrease in Cdl is probably due to a decrease in the local dielectric constant (ε) and/or an increase in the thickness (d) of the electrical double layer at the mild steel surface. This suggests that water molecules with a higher dielectric constant are replaced by inhibitor molecules with a lower dielectric constant, thereby enhancing the corrosion resistance of mild steel. The EIS spectra for the Nyquist plots comprised a significant capacitive loop at higher frequencies and an additional feature at lower frequencies (Kaya et al., 2023c). The capacitive loop at higher frequencies arises from the charge transfer resistance, surface inhomogeneity, and the time constant of the double layer. In the absence and presence of low inhibitor concentrations, a semicircular loop is observed at lower frequencies, which can be attributed to the presence of unstable adsorbed intermediate compounds, such as ((FeCl)ads, and (FeCl−Inh+) ads (Salcı et al., 2022).

The experiment covered a range of temperatures, from 300 K to 340±1K, showing a consistent trend at higher temperatures that led to increased corrosion rates and reduced inhibition efficiency. The phenomena are particularly apparent in acidic environments where the evolution of H2 gas predominates because anodic and cathodic processes are accelerated by high temperatures. The impact of temperature on inhibition effectiveness is explained in Table 6 , which compares situations under which there is inhibition and those under which there is no inhibition at various doses of DHRE. Table 6 presents data indicating a clear correlation between temperature and corrosion rate. This indicates that the protective inhibitor coating on MS surfaces has been compromised, resulting in desorption at elevated temperatures (Mobin and Aslam, 2017).

Fig. 7 a and b graphically represent the Arrhenius and transition state conditions for mild steel in a 1.0 M HCl solution at various concentrations of the DHRE inhibitor, including error bars for accuracy. The activation factors are explained in Table 7 and Eq. (8) represents the expression for the Arrhenius and transition state formulas.

Within the Arrhenius equation, A symbolizes the pre-exponential constant, R stands for the M gas constant, and T represents the absolute temperature. According to the information in Table 7, the inhibited system demonstrates a greater activation energy (Ea) value of 140.545 kJ/mol, whereas the uninhibited system has a lower value of 80.892 kJ/mol. It is important to remember that a rise in the DHRE concentration leads to a noticeable rise in activation energy (Ea), which was attributed to chemical adsorption phenomena. The activation enthalpy (ΔH*) and entropy (ΔS*) were then computed using Eq. (9) in accordance with the Arrhenius equation.

Table 7 shows that the inhibited system has higher activation energy (Ea) values (140.545 kJ/mol) than the uninhibited system (80.892 kJ/mol) according to the Arrhenius equation, which takes into account A as the pre-exponential constant, R as the M gas constant, and T as the absolute temperature. This increased Ea corresponds to the higher concentration of DHRE, which is thought to be due to chemical adsorption.

The Arrhenius plot, which shows the relationship between lnʋ corr and 1000/T (as displayed in Fig. 7a), exhibits a linear correlation with a slope equivalent to (Ea/2.303R). When plotting lnʋcorr/T against 1000/T (as displayed in Fig. 7b), an erect line is created, where the slope represents (ΔH*/2.303R), and the intercept is [ln (R/Nh) + (ΔS*/2.303R)]. These graphical representations aid in determining the activation entropy (ΔS*) and activation enthalpy (ΔH*).

Without the use of an inhibitor, the activation enthalpy (ΔH*) and activation energy (Ea*) in aqueous HCl were calculated to be 15.08 kJ/mol and 80.892 kJ/mol, correspondingly. Following the introduction of the inhibitor, these levels increased to 39.06 kJ/mol and 140.54 kJ/mol, respectively. Localized corrosion at surface sites with greater Ea* is suggested by the greater Ea* and ΔH* data in the presence of the inhibitor, which point to an increased energy barrier. While ΔS* levels are linked to greater disorderliness during the inhibitor's adsorption and subsequent attachment to the mild steel surface, positive ΔH* values indicate suppressed corrosion. A raised ΔS* and enhanced solvent entropy are resulting from the displacement of H2O molecules by this adsorption (Mobin and Aslam, 2017; Garai et al., 2012).

The prevailing assumption is that the process of corrosion inhibition occurs as a result of the inhibitor constituents binding to the interface between the metal barrier and the surrounding solution. Although the corroding surface adsorption process never reaches an equilibrium state, it does so at a stable adsorption rate that is noticeably high. In this nearly static process, the thermodynamic analysis of quasi-equilibrium adsorption is justified, employing suitable equilibrium isotherms. The efficacy of the DHRE as an inhibitor hinge on its capacity to adhere to the metal barrier. Gaining insight into the manner of adsorption is essential for understanding the correlation between the inhibitor and the metal barrier. The application of the Langmuir adsorption isotherm to experimental data insights into accumulation of the DHRE on MS in aqueous acid at a temperature of 300±1K. The Langmuir isotherm offers the most accurate match and exhibits impressive agreement with the given Eq. (10).
(8)

The Langmuir adsorption isotherm equation achieved remarkable success in fitting the model, with C representing concentration, Ɵ denoting surface area coverage, and Kads as the adsorption equilibrium constant. The effectiveness of utilizing the Langmuir adsorption isotherm to study inhibitor constituent's adsorption on MS surfaces is demonstrated by the correlation factor (R2) and a slope value close to unity. This implies that the molecules that have been adsorbed do not interact with one another. Table 8 presents the adsorption equilibrium constants (Kads) derived from the linear plots of inhibitor concentration (Cinh) versus the ratio of inhibitor concentration to surface coverage (Cinh/θ), as depicted in Fig. 8 a, which includes error bars for accuracy. These findings suggest a stronger and more efficient interconnection between the metal barrier and the DHRE compounds under investigation. Additionally, Table 8 furnishes the data for the standard free energy of adsorption (G°ads), which is linked to Kads by a pertinent Eqs. (11) and (12) (Bahrami et al., 2010).

Investigations into the interactions between the DHRE and the analysis of adsorption-free energy (ΔGo ads) on the barrier of MS often demand careful consideration. The connection between ΔGo ads and the equilibrium constant for the adsorption process (Kads) is described by the above equation. Here R denotes the gas constant, T the temperature, and CH2O the water content. To find the enthalpy of adsorption (ΔHo ads), the slope data of ΔGo ads/T vs. 1000/T were used in Fig. 8 b. Furthermore, Eq. (13) is utilized to compute the entropy of adsorption (ΔSo ads) as follows:

The adsorption process's thermodynamic parameters can be calculated using the above formulas. These metrics give crucial details regarding the strength and intensity of the DHRE's interaction with the metal barrier at different temperatures. Table 8 displays the determined values of ΔGo ads and Kads, where the 1000 value is the amount of water in the solution expressed in grams per litter. A ΔGo ads value below zero suggests that the accumulation of the DHRE onto the MS barrier occurs spontaneously. By applying this equation (9), determined value of the ΔGo ads is −26.746 kJ/mol. The negative ΔGo ads value indicates that DH root extract molecules spontaneously self-adsorb onto the mild steel surface. This behavior is consistent with the general tendency of organic inhibitors to self-adsorb on metal surfaces. If the ΔGo ads value is greater than −40 kJ/mol, chemical adsorption is considered predominant, whereas if it is less than −20 kJ/mol, physical adsorption is dominant. Values of ΔGo ads between these ranges indicate that both adsorption processes may occur. However, it is important to note that the adsorption of organic molecules on metal surfaces often involves a combination of both chemical and physical interactions. The ΔGo ads value for DH root extract is between these two extremes but closer to −20 kJ/mol, indicating that the adsorption involves both chemical and physical interactions, with physical interactions being more prominent initially (Kaya et al., 2023d). The initial stage in the adsorption of organic molecules from the extract involves their physical interactions with the electrode surface. These interactions occur through weak forces, such as dipole-dipole interactions. Beyond physisorption, chemical reactions may also take place. Chemical interactions can occur through charge sharing between the d orbitals of the metal and the free electron pairs of adsorption-active centres, such as nitrogen, sulfur, and oxygen atoms, as well as the π electrons of double or triple bonds in organic molecules (Ji et al., 2012). These findings indicate that the accumulation of the inhibitor on the MS barrier involves a combination of physical and chemical adsorption characteristics. This underscores the intricate nature of the adsorption process, highlighting the contribution of various mechanisms to corrosion inhibition (Tian et al., 2018).

The FT-IR spectrometer is an influential tool utilized to discern the bonding nature of organic inhibitors adhered to metal surfaces. FTIR spectra are utilized for scrutinizing the protective layer established on metal surfaces (Kalaivani et al., 2009; Rajendran et al., 1997). Initial quantitative phytochemical screening indicates the existence of various phytochemical compounds, including saponins, phenols, steroids, flavonoids, anthraquinones, tannins, glycosides, and alkaloids (Farzana et al., 2021). The suppression observed in the DHRE could be attributed to the existence of these primary compounds, which possess functional groups as illustrated in Fig. 9 a and 9b. In this scenario, the inhibition mechanism likely stems from the accumulation of the inhibitor onto the MS barrier, creating a safeguard and cohesive thin barrier.

The infrared spectrum of pure DHRE is depicted in Fig. 9a. In this, infrared band of –OH detected at 3782 cm−1, C–H stretching frequency of alkene at 2919 cm−1, CO band of conjugated acid at 1701 cm−1, CC stretching band of disubstituted alkene at 1655 cm−1, alcoholic –OH at 1384 cm−1, strong C–O stretching frequency at 1071 cm−1, strong C–Cl band of halo compound at 833 cm−1, and strong C–Br stretching frequency of halo compound at 597 cm−1.

The FT-IR spectrum of the MS Specimen after being immersed in an aqueous acid for 6 h, which includes 200 ppm of DHRE, is illustrated in Fig. 9b. It showed the stretching band of –OH at 3750 cm−1, C–H stretching at 2918 cm−1, CO stretching vibration at 1701 cm−1, CC stretching band at 1634 cm−1, alcoholic –OH at 1384 cm−1, C–O vibration band at 1054 cm−1, C–Cl band at 834 cm−1, C–Br stretching frequency of halo compound at 601 cm−1. The fluctuations seen in the stretching bands of disubstituted CC alkene, stretching band of –OH and C–O centres suggest that, only these electron source centres of DHRE constituents engage in interactions with the metallic substrate. The heteroatoms that make up the inhibitor molecules, as determined by FT-IR measurements, will act as Lewis bases and coat the metal substrate to prevent corrosion.

The SEM, linked with EDS, is the best tool for acquiring insights into the inhibitor's effectiveness in safeguarding electrode barriers from significant harm and promoting the development of shielding layers on metal substrates. The examination was carried out on specimens immersed in acidic solutions, with the addition and non-addition of the DHRE for a period of 24 h. Fig. 10 a, b, and 10c showcase a sequence of SEM visuals. Fig. 10a illustrates the polished mild steel surface, exhibiting clarity and highlighting the consistent and flawless quality of the metal sample. In contrast, Fig. 10b depicts the MS specimen exposed to an aggressive environment (i.e., lacking inhibitor), revealing superficial degradation and the presence of cavities resulting from the aggressive acidic medium attack. The MS specimen suspended in the acidic solution containing the optimal inhibitor concentration of 200 ppm of DHRE is represented in Fig. 10c. The metal superficial shows noticeably reduced damage, attributed to the formation of corrosion products formed amid the iron atoms and the DHRE constituents, serving as a safeguard. Furthermore, this safeguard decelerates the corrosion mechanism by obstructing additional acid solution attacks on the MS substrate, as a result diminishing the corrosion rate (Shahini et al., 2021).

Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) reveals the elemental composition (%) of the iron (Fe) samples. Sample a, which was polished, exhibits a composition of 96.61%. Sample b, which was immersed in an acid solution, shows a composition of 90.64%. On the other hand, sample c, which was also immersed in an acid solution but included the inhibitor, has a chemical composition of 93.44%. This data indicates that the inhibitor present in sample C has significantly increased its composition compared to sample B, confirming the efficiency of the DHRE on the barrier of the specimens.

AFM stands out as an invaluable instrument for comprehending surface morphology across nano-to-microscales, as well as for evaluating inhibitor effectiveness at metal-solution interfaces. AFM was used for post-experiment analysis of the surface morphology. To systematically examine the superficial texture of MS with the existence and non-existence of the DHRE inhibitors, we have captured AFM topographic images, displayed in Fig. 11 a, b, and c, along with the corresponding average surface roughness (Ra). Fig. 11a displays an AFM image of a highly smoothened MS substrate, which shows the least Ra value of 171 nm. Fig. 11b displays the AFM image of the MS sample without the DHRE inhibitor in a corrosive environment, revealing a notably elevated Ra value of 1048 nm. In Fig. 11c, the AFM image depicts the mild steel surface when exposed to an optimal concentration of 200 ppm of DHRE inhibitor. The observation explains the significant improvement in surface smoothness, as evidenced by the reduction in the value of Ra to 682 nm. These findings indicate that the acid environment caused serious harm to the MS substrate when the DHRE was absent. However, in the existence of the DHRE, the acid environment only caused minor damage to the MS barrier. This demonstrates that the DHRE's active components are intended to adhere to the MS barrier, reducing the corrosion rate (Kalkhambkar et al., 2022).

To assess how the mild steel surface behaves in terms of its hydrophobic and hydrophilic properties, a contact angle experiment was conducted with and without the DHRE. Fig. 12 a depicts the samples dipped in the aggressive solution without the inhibitor at about 6 h, displaying a contact angle of 17.1°, indicating its hydrophilic nature. The contact angle in Fig. 12b is 104.6° at the ideal concentration of 200 ppm of DHRE. The increase in contact angle values suggests that the hydrophilic character of the mild steel surface is reduced. This alteration is caused by DHRE's organic components sticking to the mild steel surface and obstructing its active areas. Consequently, the rise in hydrophobicity points to a drop in mild steel corrosion rates.

Many researchers have investigated a variety of natural corrosion inhibitors for mild steel. Some noteworthy examples are highlighted below, detailing their inhibition efficiency (%ηw), optimal concentration, and operating temperature. Among these, DHRE stands out with an impressive inhibition efficiency of 98.99%, surpassing the inhibitors listed in Table 9 .

ce:list-item.ce:para: Considerable investigation has been conducted using diverse approaches to examine the corrosion inhibitive characteristics of the DHRE inhibitor, aiming to mitigate the corrosion of mild steel in a 1M HCl solution
The inhibition efficiency was thoroughly assessed using the mass reduction method, potentiodynamic polarization, and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy techniques.
Electrochemical analysis revealed that DHRE functions as a potent corrosion inhibitor and inhibition efficiency increases with increase in DHRE concentration.
The maximum inhibition efficiency obtained from the weight loss method, polarization technique, and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy were 96.73%, 97.35%, and 97.19%, respectively.
According to Tafel graphs, DHRE acts as a mixed-type inhibitor, regulating the corrosion rate of both cathodic and anodic reactions.
EIS studies unveiled a rise in charge transfer resistance in the presence of DHRE.

The chemical features of DHRE were analyzed using the GC-MS approach, which facilitated the recognition of 43 constituents with structural features conducive to accumulation on the specimen surface and the formation of a protective layer.
Analysis using FT-IR, SEM-EDX, and AFM indicated that DHRE safeguards the mild steel surface by creating a protective layer on the specimen surface, consistent with the electrochemical data.
The WCA evaluation illustrated that DHRE constituents render the specimen surface hydrophobic.

ce:section.ce:para: In conclusion, DHRE exhibits excellent corrosion inhibition properties for MS in a 1M HCl solution, making it a viable candidate for industrial applications where sustainable and effective corrosion protection is required.

Savitri Danappa Kotabagi: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation. Ragini L. Minagalavar: Writing – original draft, Software, Conceptualization.
